text: Using a robotic approach, the surgeon divides minimal adhesions to visualize the hernia defect located deep in the lesser pelvis and perineum.
The robotic approach is utilized to access the deep lesser pelvis. Minimal adhesions are divided, revealing the hernia defect located deep in the perineum.

The surgical team sets up the robotic ports similar to a lower anterior resection, with the patient positioned in Trendelenburg and slightly tilted to the right. The robotic system is docked parallel to the patient's left leg.

The surgical team sets up the robotic ports similar to a lower anterior resection, with the patient positioned in Trendelenburg and slightly tilted to the right. The robotic system is docked parallel to the patient's left leg.

The surgeon lifts the uterus and vagina to reveal the perineal defect below the levator muscles. The posterior part of the vagina is visible. The operation begins with a levatorplasty using interrupted sutures to close the defect by utilizing the levator plates. After closing the levator, a small gap remains between the vagina and the levatorplasty, which is closed by stitching the posterior vaginal wall onto the levators.

The surgeon lifts the uterus and vagina to reveal the perineal defect located below the levator muscles, which is causing the patient's symptoms. The posterior part of the vagina is visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon performs a levatorplasty using interrupted sutures to close the perineal defect, utilizing the levator plates. After closure, a small gap remains between the vagina and the levatorplasty, which is closed by stitching the posterior vaginal wall onto the levators.

The surgeon measures the perineal defect and cuts a Phasix ST mesh to the appropriate dimensions. The mesh is introduced through the assistance port and positioned within the defect, with markings indicating the side that will lie adjacent to the bowel loops.
The surgeon measures the perineal defect and cuts a Phasix ST mesh to the appropriate dimensions. The mesh is then introduced into the surgical field through the assistance port.

The surgeon marks the mesh with letters as advised by the manufacturer, indicating the side that will lie adjacent to the bowel loops. The mesh is positioned directly over the defect, and the plan is to secure it with interrupted sutures at the pelvic brim, the edge of the posterior vagina, and the pelvic sidewalls on both sides.

The surgeon plans to suture the Phasix ST mesh with interrupted sutures at the pelvic brim, the edge of the posterior vagina, and the pelvic sidewalls on both sides. The mesh is positioned to lie in direct contact with the small bowel loops.
The surgeon uses the robotic platform to place interrupted sutures in the lesser pelvis, carefully avoiding a visible pulsating vessel beneath the planned suture site.
The surgeon uses the robotic platform to place interrupted sutures in the lesser pelvis, carefully avoiding a visible pulsating vessel beneath the planned suture site.

The surgeon stitches the Phasix ST mesh at the pelvic sidewalls, pelvic brim, and posterior wall of the vagina, closing the gap between the vagina and levatorplasty. After securing the mesh, water is applied to activate the bioabsorbable properties of the mesh.

The surgeon stitches the Phasix ST mesh at the pelvic sidewalls, pelvic brim, and posterior wall of the vagina, closing the gap left after levatorplasty. Water is applied to activate the bioabsorbable mesh.
